messages.content:
**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

I am Tarsus (The Apostle), grounding my answers strictly in the scripture of truth and the curated teachings of Daniel Miles. How can I assist you in dividing the word of truth today?

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**

> **Thinking Process:**
> 
> The user asks "what is perfection for God?" This is a profound theological question that requires careful analysis from the provided documents. The user's profile shows interest in Christology and soteriology, so I need to connect this to the progression from law to grace. The "Perfection" study document is the primary source here, and it explicitly equates "perfect" with "merciful" by cross-referencing Matthew 5:48 with Luke 6:36. The document also connects perfection to the "God of all grace" in 1 Peter 5:10, and to the concept of "mercy void of sacrifice." The "God of all grace is not law" email reinforces that God is not both law and grace, but all grace. The kingdomstoryfilms documents about giving God what is of God provide a parallel about bearing God's image. I must avoid placing God "under" anything, and instead speak of God's nature as pure grace, mercy, and peace. The response must be highly detailed, use abundant inline citations, and draw from multiple documents. I will structure the answer around: 1) Perfection defined as mercy (Matthew 5:48 = Luke 6:36), 2) Perfection as grace void of law, 3) Perfection as peace void of confusion, 4) The God of all grace as the source of perfection, 5) The image of God we bear as the call to reflect this perfection. I will use footnotes from the Perfection study, the God of all grace email, and the kingdomstoryfilms documents.

**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

Perfection for God is not a legal demand of flawlessness, as the cOLD covenant law would require, but rather the very nature of His being as pure mercy, grace, and peace. The scripture reveals this through a precise cross-reference: "Be ye therefore perfect, even as your Father which is in heaven is perfect" in Matthew 5:48 is directly equated with "Be ye therefore merciful, as your Father also is merciful" in Luke 6:36 [^1]. This is not a coincidence but a deliberate textual connection showing that God's perfection *is* His mercy. The law may demand perfection as a standard of performance, but the law is neither perfect nor merciful; it cannot make any comers thereunto perfect [^1]. Therefore, God's perfection is not a standard to be attained by human effort or sacrifice, but a quality of His character that He *is*—merciful, gracious, and full of compassion.

Furthermore, perfection for God is the state of being "the God of all grace," which means He is not law, nor both law and grace, but *all* grace [^2]. The reasoning is that God is not the author of confusion, but of peace [^2]. To attribute both law (which worketh wrath, Rom 4:15) and grace (which brings life) to God would be a bi-polar oxymoron, creating confusion rather than peace. Perfection, then, is the absence of this contradiction; it is grace void of law, mercy void of sacrifice, and peace void of confusion [^1]. This is why the scripture declares that God "will have mercy, and not sacrifice" (Hosea 6:6, Matthew 9:13, 12:7)—sacrifice belongs to the law, which is a dead-end system, while mercy is the very essence of the perfect God [^1]. To add sacrifice to mercy is as adding law to grace, which is as adding death to life.

This perfection is also the destination of the journey "through Jesus > Christ > to God" as described in 2 Corinthians 5: be ye reconciled to God [^1]. The journey begins with the temporal salvation of Jesus (the Son of man) and progresses to the eternal salvation of Christ (the end of the law), but the final destination is *that God*—the God of all grace, who is light void of darkness [^1]. This is the "perfection" spoken of in Hebrews 6:1, where we are exhorted to "go on unto perfection" by leaving the principles of the doctrine of Christ, which includes leaving behind the temporary and partial salvation by sacrifice [^1]. Christ, being "made perfect" (merciful), became the author of *eternal* salvation, not temporal salvation [^1]. Thus, God's perfection is the eternal, unchanging state of pure grace, which is the source and guarantee of our own perfection in Him.

The image of God stamped upon humanity, as illustrated in the kingdomstoryfilms teaching, further clarifies this. Just as Caesar's image on a coin declared his ownership and reign, so God's image in us declares that we are His possession, bearing His likeness [^3]. The question "give God what is of God" is a call to reflect His nature—to be perfect as He is perfect, which is to be merciful as He is merciful [^3]. This is not a legal obligation but a natural outflow of knowing that we are made in His image and are being conformed to that image through the God of all grace, who makes us perfect, stablishes, strengthens, and settles us (1 Peter 5:10) [^1]. Perfection for God, therefore, is not a distant, unattainable ideal, but the very reality of His being—merciful, gracious, and peaceful—which He invites us to enter into and reflect.

Learn More:
- [Perfection](/pages/perfection)
- [God of all grace is not law, nor both law & grace, but all grace](/pages/god_of_all_grace_is_not_law_nor_both_law_&_grace_but_all_grace)
- [kingdomstoryfilms-give-God-whats-is-of-Gods(v2)](/pages/tt_unknown_kingdomstoryfilms_give_god_whats_is_of_gods_v2_20221215)

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**

1.title: Perfection
1.snippet: Be ye therefore perfect in Mt 5:48 is be ye therefore merciful: Lk 6:36; so Grace and Peace not full of Mercy is vain religion. ... The law may demand perfection: be thou perfect, but law is neither perfect nor merciful. ... Perfection is not possible via the law; Rather it's only possible via Grace void of law. ... God is not the author of confusion, but is the author of peace. Pure wisdom is not both merciful and merciless, but full of mercy, only. Grace unto you is not both grace and law, but the God of all grace (no law). ... The journey is through Jesus > Christ. The destination is God, specifically that God. ... Christ, being made perfect (merciful), became the author of eternal salvation, not the author of temporal salvation.

2.title: God of all grace is not law, nor both law & grace, but all grace
2.snippet: God of all grace is not law, nor both law & grace, but all grace. Reasoning: God isn't both Law & Grace, but Law or Grace. Why? Both would be a bi-polar oxy-moron: 2 contrary things that can't co-exist in peace; as if confusion, not peace. So Paul reasons 'God is not [author] of confusion, but of peace'.

3.title: kingdomstoryfilms-give-God-whats-is-of-Gods(v2)
3.snippet: Un rey graba su imagen en sus monedas para mostrar que él las posee... Jesús estaba señalando que Dios estampó su imagen en nosotros. ... Tú llevas la imagen de Dios. Dale a Dios lo que es de Dios.
